data.title: Interactive Brokers Group 4Q Profit, Sales Rise on Higher Trading Volumes
data.description:
By Kelly Cloonan

Interactive Brokers Group logged higher profit and sales in its latest quarter as trading volume rose across stocks, options and futures.

The online brokerage platform on Tuesday posted a fourth-quarter profit of $284 million, or 63 cents a share, compared with $217 million, or 50 cents a share, a year earlier.

Adjusted earnings per share were 65 cents, ahead of estimates of 59 cents a share, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

Revenue rose to $1.64 billion from $1.39 billion, in line with analyst projections.

Commission revenue rose 22% to $582 million on higher customer trading volumes. Trading volumes in options, futures and stocks increased 27%, 22% and 16%, respectively.

Customer accounts increased by 32% to 4.4 million, with customer equity up 37% to $779.9 billion.

Write to Kelly Cloonan at kelly.cloonan@wsj.com

data.title: Zions Bancorporation Reports Strong Q4 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
Zions Bancorporation, N.A., a prominent financial services company operating in 11 western states, has released its fourth-quarter 2025 financial results. The company reported significant growth in key financial metrics, reflecting robust performance and strategic initiatives aimed at enhancing shareholder value.

Financial Highlights

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Zions Bancorporation reported net earnings of $262 million, translating to a diluted earnings per share (EPS) of $1.76. This marks a notable increase from the $200 million in net earnings and $1.34 diluted EPS reported in the same quarter of the previous year. The annual net earnings for 2025 stood at $895 million, with a diluted EPS of $6.01, compared to $737 million and $4.95, respectively, in 2024.

Net interest income for the quarter was $683 million, up 9% from the previous year, driven by lower funding costs and a favorable shift in the composition of average interest-earning assets. The net interest margin improved to 3.31%, compared to 3.05% in the prior year period.

Business and Operational Highlights

Pre-provision net revenue (PPNR) for the quarter was $356 million, a 10% increase from the previous year. Adjusted PPNR was $331 million, up 6%. Customer-related noninterest income rose by 1% to $177 million, while noninterest expense increased by 7% to $546 million. Adjusted noninterest expense was $548 million, up 8%, primarily due to a $15 million charitable contribution during the quarter.

Loans and leases grew by 3% to $60.9 billion, with a significant reduction in the annualized ratio of net loan and lease charge-offs to average loans and leases, which dropped to 0.05% from 0.24%. The provision for credit losses was $6 million, down from $41 million in the previous year.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Zions Bancorporation's capital position strengthened, with the estimated Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio rising to 11.5% from 10.9%. Tangible book value per common share increased by 21% to $40.79. The company also reversed an FDIC Special Assessment accrual of $9 million, contributing positively to the financial results.

In terms of deposits, total deposits were $75.6 billion, down 1% from the previous year, while customer deposits (excluding brokered deposits) increased by 1% to $71.8 billion. Short-term borrowings decreased by 19% to $3.1 billion.

Management's Perspective

Harris H. Simmons, Chairman and CEO of Zions Bancorporation, expressed satisfaction with the fourth-quarter results, highlighting a 31% increase in earnings per share compared to the previous year. He noted the positive operating leverage achieved during the quarter and the strong credit quality, with annualized net charge-offs totaling 0.05% of loans. Simmons also emphasized the company's strengthened capital position and the continued growth in loans and deposits.

Future Outlook

Looking ahead, Zions Bancorporation anticipates continued prudent growth in 2026. The company aims to build on the strong performance of 2025, with a focus on maintaining robust credit quality, enhancing capital strength, and achieving positive operating leverage.

Original SEC Filing: ZIONS BANCORPORATION, NATIONAL ASSOCIATION /UT/ [ ZION ] - 8-K - Jan. 20, 2026

Disclaimer

data.title: United Airlines offers upbeat outlook on strong premium travel demand
data.description:
United Airlines on Tuesday issued an upbeat outlook for the current quarter and the full year, buoyed by strong demand from higher‑income and corporate travelers.

The Chicago‑based carrier also exceeded profit expectations for the December quarter.

United forecast first-quarter adjusted profit of $1 to $1.50 per share. The midpoint of the range, $1.25, is above analysts' average estimate of $1.13, according to LSEG data.

For 2026, United projected adjusted earnings of $12 to $14 per share, compared with analysts' average estimate of $13.16 a share.

Its adjusted earnings for the fourth quarter came in at $3.10 per share, topping analysts' estimate of $2.94. Total revenue rose 4.8% to $15.4 billion.

data.title: US stocks battered amid building uncertainty
data.description:
Key points:
  • Main US indexes end sharply lower; Nasdaq off most, down ~2.4%
  • Tech, Cons Disc weakest S&P 500 sectors; Staples sole gainer
  • Dollar tumbles; bitcoin down >3.5%; crude, gold both up >1.5%
  • US 10-Year Treasury yield jumps to ~4.29%

US STOCKS BATTERED AMID BUILDING UNCERTAINTY

The main U.S. stock indexes tumbled on Tuesday, as investors returned from the U.S. long weekend and reacted negatively to fresh tariff threats from President Donald Trump against Europe and renewed volatility in global markets.

The risk-off wave helped vault gold to fresh record highs, knocked stocks lower globally and left U.S. Treasuries wobbling under renewed selling pressure.

Indeed, the PHLX Gold/Silver Index (.XAU) was able to score yet another record close on Tuesday. This, while the Dow slid more than 1.7% and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite both tumbled more than 2% each.

The U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield hit 4.313%, or its highest level since August 22 of last year. The yield rise was more pronounced on the long end of the curve, as investors not only reacted to President Trump's renewed threat of a trade clash with Europe over its proposed acquisition of Greenland, but also to turbulence in Japanese government bonds.

With the day's instability, the Nasdaq finished below its 50-day moving average (DMA) for the first time since January 2 of this year, while the S&P 500 ended below its 50-DMA for the first time since December 17.

Nearly all S&P 500 sectors ended red with Tech and Consumer Discretionary taking the biggest hits. Just defensive Staples edged into the green at close.

All seven members of the Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF ended down on the day. The ETF lost 3% for its biggest one-day drop since October 30. However, in another pocket of some strength, the Nasdaq Biotech Index ended modestly higher.

In any event, despite the day's carnage, recent relative performance trends persisted. Value held up better than Growth (.IGX), and the S&P 500 Equal Weighted Index (.SPXEW) fell less than the cap-weighted index.

Although the small-cap Russell 2000 lost 1.2%, it also held up better than the SPX. Tuesday marked the 12th day in a row that this small-cap index has outperformed the S&P 500. The last streak of small-cap outperformance that was longer was a 13-day run that ended in June 2008.

Meanwhile, amid bubbling uncertainty, the CBOE Volatility Index is on pace to end above 20.00 for the first time since November 24.

Here is a snapshot of where markets stood just shortly after 4 p.m. ET.

data.title: Los Angeles to Sell $544.3 Million of Power System Revenue Bonds
data.description:
By Chris Wack

The Department of Water and Power of the City of Los Angeles plans to sell $544.3 million of power system revenue bonds to help pay for the costs of system improvements.

The department also plans to use the money from the sale to refund certain outstanding bonds of the department, payable from the Power Revenue Fund, according to documents posted on MuniOS.

Retail orders can be place on Jan. 21, and institutional pricing is scheduled for Jan. 22. Closing is set for Feb. 10.

Interest on the Series 2025 D bonds is payable semiannually starting on July 1.

The bonds mature starting on July 1 through 2056. Rates and yields have not yet been determined.

The bonds will be special obligations of the department, payable only from the Power Revenue Fund and not out of any other fund or money of the department or the city of Los Angeles.

The Department of Water and Power of the City of Los Angeles is the largest municipal utility in the U.S., and has more than 1.5 million power customers, according the roadshow document posted on MuniOS. It provides electric and water service to an area consisting almost entirely of the city. It is governed by the Board of Water and Power Commissioners of the City of Los Angeles.

Moody's has rated the bonds Aa2, and Fitch Ratings has rated the bonds AA-.

Siebert Williams Shank and BofA Securities are senior managers on the sale.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: US VP JD Vance and Usha Vance to welcome fourth child in July
data.description:
U.S. Vice President JD Vance and his wife, Usha, will welcome their fourth child, a boy, in July, the couple said in a joint statement on Tuesday.

Usha Vance announced the news on the second lady's X account, where the pair said she and the baby were "doing well."

"During this exciting and hectic time, we are particularly grateful for the military doctors who take excellent care of our family and for the staff members who do so much to ensure that we can serve the country while enjoying a wonderful life with our children," the Vances said.

JD Vance, an Ohio native, and Usha, the daughter of Indian immigrants, met while attending Yale Law School, where they graduated in 2013.

Their first son, Ewan, was born in 2017, followed by another son, Vivek, in 2020. The couple's daughter, Mirabel, was born in 2021.

data.title: US subpoenas Minnesota leaders in criminal grand jury probe over opposition to immigration crackdown
data.description:
Key points:
  • Subpoenas target Minnesota leaders opposing Trump's immigration enforcement
  • Federal agents' actions in Minneapolis have sparked protests
  • Minnesota Democrats call investigation politically motivated

By Andrew Hay and Jana Winter

The U.S. Department of Justice served grand jury subpoenas to the offices of Minnesota's governor and attorney general, and the mayor of Minneapolis, as it weighs whether their public opposition to President Donald Trump's surge in immigration enforcement amounts to a crime.

One of the subpoenas, shared by Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey, orders his office's custodian of records to produce documents since the beginning of 2025 related to "cooperation or lack of cooperation with federal immigration authorities." Six offices were served with subpoenas, according to a Justice Department official, including those of Governor Tim Walz and Attorney General Keith Ellison.

Trump, a Republican, has sent thousands of immigration agents into the Minneapolis area in recent weeks in a highly visible, sometimes violent surge that has little modern precedent. The agents have carried guns through the city's snowy streets, dressed in military-style camouflage gear and masks, drawing loud protests from residents. Trump administration officials have blamed Walz and Frey for the protests, accusations they deny.

The hostility by many residents has only deepened after one agent fatally shot an American woman, Renee Good, in her car nearly two weeks ago. Federal agents have used tear gas and other chemical irritants on protesters, and have drawn outrage for questioning or arresting Black, Latino or Asian U.S. citizens, including a man who was wrongly arrested and pulled out of his home on Sunday dressed in underpants and sandals.

The subpoenas arrived a few days after it became public that the U.S. Justice Department was investigating Walz, Frey and other Democrats and outspoken critics of Trump.

DEMOCRATS HAVE CALLED FOR CALM

Democratic politicians in Minnesota have called for calm, but have also been sharply critical of what they see as a politically motivated crackdown by Trump, and have sued his administration, asking a judge last week to order an end to what they call the unconstitutional excesses of his enforcement surge.

Walz called the federal investigation "a partisan distraction" that "does not seek justice."

"Minnesotans are more concerned with safety and peace rather than with baseless legal tactics aimed at intimidating public servants standing shoulder to shoulder with their community," his statement said.

Frey said in a statement that the federal government was trying "to intimidate local leaders for doing their jobs" and that "every American should be concerned."

"We shouldn't live in a country where federal law enforcement is used to play politics or crack down on local voices they disagree with," he said.

data.title: Mergers and acquisitions
data.description:
The following bids, mergers, acquisitions and disposals were reported by 20:45 GMT on Tuesday:

** Italian media group GEDI has entered into exclusive talks with regional publisher Gruppo SAE to sell the Italian newspaper La Stampa.

** Qiagen is weighing strategic options, including a potential sale of the company amid fresh takeover interest, Bloomberg News reported, citing people with knowledge of the matter.

** Kuwaiti businessman Bader Nasser Al‑Kharafi said on Tuesday he had acquired a 3% stake in luxury yacht maker Ferretti , amid moves to reshape the Italian company's shareholder base and board.

** Palantir leaders said the U.S. technology company has clinched a deal to sell more software to HD Hyundai 267250.KS, ramping up its heavy-industry work in South Korea.

** Risk Theory has set its sights on further M&A following its recent deal for Jet Insurance Company as it also targets talent acquisition to support its entry into new market segments.

** Restaurant Brands Asia said that Inspira Global will acquire a controlling stake in the Burger King India and Indonesia operator for around 4.60 billion rupees ($50.54 million).

** Britain's GSK has agreed to buy RAPT Therapeutics for $2.2 billion, accessing the U.S. biotech's experimental drug to treat food allergies as it seeks new medicines ahead of patent losses for a top-selling HIV drug.

** Netflix has switched to an all-cash offer for Warner Bros Discovery's studio and streaming assets without increasing the $82.7 billion price in a bid to shut the door on Paramount's rival efforts to snag the Hollywood giant.

** Abivax Chief Executive Marc de Garidel dismissed as "noise" reports in the French press that Eli Lilly may take over his company, and said market speculation about an ongoing review by French authorities was inconsistent with how French foreign-investment oversight works.

** Acrisure has completed the rebrand of two of its UK insurance broking acquisitions, bringing heritage brokers Russell Scanlan and WH&R McCartney under the Acrisure name, the broker said.

** Universal Music Group is set to secure EU antitrust approval for its $775 million bid for Downtown Music after regulators signalled acceptance of its offer to sell the latter's royalty services platform, people with direct knowledge of the matter said.

** New Zealand's Fletcher Building said on Tuesday it will sell its construction division to a unit of French infrastructure firm VINCI SA for NZ$315.6 million ($182.76 million).

** Zurich Insurance Group has made a 7.67 billion pound ($10.3 billion) offer for speciality insurer Beazley , going public with an improved bid that sent shares in the British company up 40% on Monday.

** Italian multi‑utility firm Hera said on Monday it had agreed to acquire water treatment group Sostelia in a deal worth 138 million euros ($161 million) including debt, boosting its position in the treatment of industrial and civil water.

** Mexican conglomerate Grupo Carso , controlled by magnate Carlos Slim, will buy out Lukoil to hold a full stake in the Ichalkil and Pokoch offshore oil fields, the firm said in a filing on Monday.

** Goldman Sachs-backed UK broker consolidator Clear Group has made its first acquisition of 2026 with a deal to buy insurance broker Shire Insurance Services Limited (SIS).

** Specialty MGA Optio Group on Monday announced that it has completed its acquisition of the remaining 75% stake in Italian MGA Heca S.r.l for an undisclosed sum.

** Private equity firm Everstone will sell its entire 11.26% stake in Burger King's India and Indonesia franchisee Restaurant Brands Asia , as part of a deal to be announced on Tuesday, two people with direct knowledge of the matter said.

data.title: Longer-Term Treasury Yields Rise as Trump Turns Up the Heat on Greenland — Market Talk
data.description: Long-term Treasurys sell off as Trump's assertive foreign policy adds long-term risk while Fed officials diverge about how fast interest rates could fall. Tomorrow, the Supreme Court begins hearing arguments on the potential firing of Fed Governor Cook. The Fed is expected to hit pause next week and markets are mostly pricing two cuts this year, according to CME data. December pending home sales tomorrow are forecast to slow, in a WSJ consensus. The 10-year yield adds 0.064 percentage point to 4.294%, its highest level since August, and the 30-year rises 0.081 p.p. to 4.920%, the highest since September. The two-year falls slightly, to 3.596%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Standoff with Iran over inspections cannot go on forever, IAEA chief says
data.description:
Key points:
  • IAEA has inspected all sites not bombed, Grossi says
  • Agency still has not accessed key facilities that were bombed
  • Not feasible to inspect during social unrest, Grossi says
  • Resolving standoff this spring is 'reasonable time frame'

By Dave Graham and Francois Murphy

The standoff with Iran over accounting for its stock of highly enriched uranium and inspecting nuclear facilities bombed by the United States and Israel cannot go on forever, U.N. nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi said on Tuesday.

The International Atomic Energy Agency has inspected all 13 declared nuclear facilities in Iran that were not bombed, but has been unable to inspect any of the three key sites that were bombed in June - Natanz, Fordow or Isfahan - Grossi told Reuters in an interview at the World Economic Forum in Davos.

Iran must first file a report to the IAEA on what happened to those sites and material, including an estimated 440.9 kg of uranium enriched to up to 60% purity, close to the roughly 90% weapons-grade level. That is enough material, if enriched further, for 10 nuclear bombs, according to an IAEA yardstick.

NO 'A LA CARTE' OPTION

Iran has not submitted that special report to the IAEA.

"This cannot go on forever because at some point, I will have to say, 'Well, I don't have any idea where this material is,'" which would mean there was no guarantee the material had not been diverted or hidden, Grossi said.

"I do not have that conviction or conclusion at the moment, but what we are saying to Iran is that they need to engage."

Iran says it is fully cooperating with the IAEA. Its government could not immediately be reached for comment.

It is now at least seven months since the IAEA last verified Iran's stock of highly enriched uranium. Its own guidance is that it should be done monthly.

Grossi said he was exercising "diplomatic prudence," but that Iran had to meet its obligations as a party to the nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty.

"This cannot go on like this for a long time without me, unfortunately, having to declare them in non-compliance," he said, noting that parties to the NPT do not have an "a la carte" option where they can pick and choose what to comply with.

Asked if the issue could be resolved this spring, he said: "That is a reasonable time frame."

LATEST INSPECTIONS WERE IN DECEMBER

One of the "real world" realities Grossi said he must face is the influence of diplomatic efforts aimed at reaching a broader agreement between Iran and the United States that have been spearheaded by U.S. special envoy Steve Witkoff.

"I cannot ignore it, and I wish it well so that there can be an understanding without the looming threat of new military activity over there or something of the sort," he said.

The IAEA said in November it had inspected most of Iran's nuclear facilities that were not struck in the U.S. and Israeli attacks. It has since carried out further inspections until late December, Grossi said.

It was not possible, however, to carry out inspections during civil unrest, he said, referring to recent protests that prompted a severe crackdown by the Islamic Republic. Iranian officials have indicated the unrest has stopped, he said.

"They said that things are calm and they are in control, etc.," Grossi said. "If this is the situation, shouldn't we resume (inspections)?"

Grossi said he would meet Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi in "a matter of days, weeks."
